title: DBSCAN: Clustering by Density
concept: DBSCAN groups points that are packed closely together into dense regions and labels points sitting alone in sparse areas as noise, all without ever specifying the number of clusters.

exercises.prompt: Recap: True or false — agglomerative clustering starts with one big cluster and splits it apart.
exercises.explain: False — that's divisive clustering. Agglomerative starts with every point alone and merges upward. Today: an algorithm that doesn't merge or split at all.

cards.title: Spotting a crowd in a park
cards.body: Picture a park from above. In some spots, people are packed shoulder-to-shoulder — clearly a crowd. In other spots, there's just one person sitting alone on a bench, far from anyone else. You don't need to count how many "groups" of people exist to see this — you just look for tightly packed areas versus isolated spots. That's exactly what DBSCAN does with data.

cards.title: What is DBSCAN?
cards.body: DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) groups together points that are packed densely within a small distance of each other. Points that don't have enough close neighbors are labeled noise — outliers that don't belong to any cluster.

cards.title: Campfires, not cookie cutters
cards.body: Instead of forcing every point into one of K predefined groups, DBSCAN is like walking through a dark field looking for campfires: wherever there's a tight cluster of light (densely packed points), that's a cluster. A single flickering candle far off in the distance, with no other lights nearby, gets marked as noise instead of forced into a group.

cards.title: Finding crime hotspots on a map
cards.body: A city analyzing where break-ins cluster on a map doesn't want to force the data into a fixed number of regions — it wants to find naturally dense hotspots, wherever they are, and ignore the rare isolated incident. DBSCAN finds those hotspots automatically and flags the lone incidents as noise instead of stretching a hotspot to include them.

cards.title: No K, and clusters can be any shape
cards.body: You might think every clustering algorithm needs a number of clusters chosen upfront, and that clusters always look like round blobs. DBSCAN needs neither: it doesn't take a K at all, and because it just follows chains of densely packed points, it can trace out oddly shaped clusters — crescents, spirals, winding streaks — that round-cluster methods would mangle.

left.text:
Many points close together
within a small distance
forms one cluster

right.text:
Too few nearby neighbors
labeled an outlier
not part of any cluster

exercises.prompt: What does DBSCAN do with a point that has very few neighbors nearby?

options.text: Labels it as noise, an outlier that isn't part of any cluster
Forces it into the nearest cluster anyway
Makes it its own required cluster

exercises.explain: Correct — DBSCAN labels sparse, isolated points as noise instead of cramming them into a cluster.

exercises.prompt: True or false: DBSCAN requires you to specify the number of clusters (K) before running.
exercises.explain: False — DBSCAN never takes a K. It just looks for densely packed regions, however many there turn out to be.

exercises.sentence: DBSCAN groups points that are ___ packed together and marks isolated points as noise.
exercises.explain: DBSCAN forms clusters wherever points are densely packed, and marks isolated points as noise.

exercises.prompt: Which clustering result is a strong sign that DBSCAN was used instead of a method that only finds round clusters?

options.text: A cluster shaped like a winding spiral, with a few scattered points left unclustered as noise
Exactly K neat, round clusters with every single point assigned to one
A single tree diagram showing every merge

exercises.explain: Right — oddly shaped clusters plus explicitly unclustered noise points are hallmarks of DBSCAN.

exercises.prompt: A ride-share company wants to find natural 'pickup hotspots' across a city from millions of GPS pings, where hotspots can be irregular shapes (like along a curved street) and rare stray pings shouldn't distort any hotspot's shape. Which approach fits best, and why?

options.text: DBSCAN, because it finds densely packed regions of any shape and marks stray pings as noise instead of forcing them into a hotspot
K-Means, because it's the fastest clustering algorithm overall
DBSCAN, because it requires picking the exact number of hotspots in advance
Hierarchical clustering, because it always produces round clusters

exercises.explain: Exactly! DBSCAN's density-based approach handles irregular hotspot shapes and cleanly separates out the stray pings as noise, with no K required. Next: putting all three algorithms side by side to decide which one to reach for.
